This is in response to your Ietter requesting further advice

""n-"i.i"q 
his duties under the conf L ict-of- interest provisions of
tfre political 
Reform Act (the "Act )1 both as a city councilmernber
and a member of the city redevelopnent agency' Your letter
requests reconsideration of the advice Ietter issued to your

4e9.)
Please note that nothing in thj's Ietter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place' In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented.to us' 
The
comnission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
iin i" oq"f=U" (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.) Finally, our advice is

We cannot provide advice
about other- confl ict-of- interest laws, such as Government code

OUESTIONS
1. If councilnember Penna participates in dj'scussions and
actions concerning the shearwater project as a city councilrnember

or member of the redevelopment agency, may the councilmember

a. Represent a client r.rho wishes to lease or purchase

c. Actively seek private business in the project area

3. May councilnember Penna be present at either open or

CONCLUS IONS
1. As concluded in the Armento Advice Letter, Councilmernber
Penna, as sol,e owner of his real estate business, has an econonic
interest in his business. consequently, ehere a decision vill
foreseeably affect the councilmenber's business, he must
disqualify hiursel,f fron the decision.
2. Participation includes connitting an agency to a course
of action, entering into a contractual agreement on behalf of the
agency, deteruining not to act, negotiating, advising or naking
reconraendations to the decision-uaker. Thus, participating- in the
city council's negotiations and Datters concerning sale or lease
of property with the developer would be participation in a
governnental decision and is prohibited if the councilrnember has a
conflict of interest.
L
3. A disqualified counciluenber nay not attend a closed
session of the city council concerning the matter for which he is
disqualified, but is not precluded fron being present at an gpc.ll
neeting. Materials presented to the city council for a closed
session of the city council nay not be provided to a councilnember
who is disqualified vrith respect to that matter. Except for
confidential rnaterial, nateriaL scheduled for discussion at an
gpC]l session of the city council need not be withheld from a
disqualified off iciaI.

Page 3
FACTS
The pertinent facts in the Armento letter are as folLows: At
the behest of a developer, the city of south san Franc j-sco created
the Shearwater redevelopment project (the "project'r) at a site
enconpassing 54 acres of dry Iand and 133 acres of water. 
A
master-planned, mixed-use development is proposed.
The city owns a six-acre parcel within the Project area.
Pursuant to an agreenent with the developer, the city anticipates
sellinq or leasing this parcel to the developer. The details of
this tiansaction are being negotiated by the city manager, subiect
to direction and ul.tinate aPproval by the city council.
At the tine the Armento Advice Letter was issued, there were
a number of pending governnental decisions concerning the project.
The developer has requested that the city establish an assessment
district 
and a tandscape and Lighting maintenance district 
for
various financing purposes. other discussions and decisions
concerning the financial feasibil-ity of the project and the terms
of the =ai" or Lease of the city-owned property were also pending-
since the project would be constructed in phases, the individual
aspects of thi project, such as the approval of al'l the buildings
(retail, office, hote1, and, possibly condorniniums) and the
specific design of the narina, will be subject to various approval
processes.
Although the devetoper has retained its own realtor to act as
the agent for marketing and Leasing the shearwater site, any
brokei nay bring a client directly to the developer and receive a
fulI corunission for the lease or sale of space in the project.
CounciLnember Penna is a real estate broker and has been the o$rner
of Penna Realty for over 26 years. Over 80 percent of his
business, which includes property nanagenent, real estate sales of
residential, cornmercial and industrial property, and real estate
appraising and consulting, is conducted in South san Francisco.
councilrnenber Penna has not been involved in any sales relating to
shearwater.
During titarch 1990, Councilme:nber Penna approached the
shearlrater developer and offered to do a presentation on the
Shearwater Project at the International Association of Realtors
(FIABCI) conference in Acapu1co, Mexico. The Shearwater developer
authorized hin to rnake a presentation and supplied hin with
rnaterials. Approximately 1,000 brokers attended the conference'
If a broker came forward as a result of the presentation and
engaged councilnember Penna to present an offer, Councilmernber
Penna rrould receive a commission. To date, no broker has cone
forward in this matter.
Your request for reconsideration included additional and
:nodif ied facts rrhich have been incorporated into thj.s response.


Our Fi■e NO. A-90-499b
Page 4
1. The City of South San Francisco has a population of
52,OOo persons. out of a total area of 9.53 square niles, L,5oo
acres in the city liurits (one-fourth of the city) is zoned
commercial and industrial, the rest is zoned residential.
2. The Shearwater property is owned by a single owner except
for the 6 acres owned by the city.
3, You emphasized that the individual components of the
Shearwater project, such as the approval of the final subdivision
map and all of the aPprovals for the buildings (retaiI, office,
hotel, possible condouiniuns) as welI as the specific design of a
marina, wil.I each be subject to separate discretionarv approval by
the city counc i I .
4. 
You nodified the facts concerning Councilmenber Penna's
participation in the Hexico conference as follor'rs: 
counciLmenber
Penna's sole association with the developer has been the
presentation of the hotel site at the conference in May 1990, and
iris authority was then concluded. councilmember Penna did not
receive any rnonetary compensation and has received no financial
gain frorn Lhe presentation. No comnissions resulted frorn the
conference.
5. You also clarified that in addition to having
participated in no sales in the project area, councilrnember Penna
iras "no- present or futurel intention of pursuino business
opportunities in the Shearwater Project. 
However, you did not
i-naicate that the councilmernber l{ould decline business fron the
project area.
6. Litigation and financial problems have delayed the
project.
ANALYSIS
The Political Reforn Act was enacted by the people of the
State of California by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the
conf I ict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act was to ensure that
public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would perfor:n
their duties in an irnpartial nanner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons rrho have supported them. (Section 81001.(b) .) 
The Act
leeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks
to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest.
(witt v. Morrow, (L977) 7O CaI.App.3d 817, 823.)
In furtherance of this goaI, Section 871-o0 of the Act
prohibits any public official 
fron rnaking, participatj.ng in
making, or otherwise using his or her official 
position to


Our File NO. A-90-499b
Page 5
influence a governnental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
As discussed in our first letter to your
office, a I'public official" 
is defined in section 82048 and
Regulation lgzoo as every natural person who j.s a mernber, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.
this definition would include both redevelopment agency rnembers
and nenbers of the south San Francisco city council- (Arnento
Advice Letter, suDra. )
Please note that a conflict of interest arises r'rith respect
to a specific qovernnental decision. The Act does not restrict
councFernber ienna's private activity. 
However, as will 
be
discussed belotr, where it is foreseeable that a governmental
decision will affect an existing economic interest of the
councilnember, even in the future, a conflict of interest arises
at the time of the decision.
1. EconaEiq_l-nleEe-glg
Section 87103 Provides:
An official 
has a financial interest in a
decision r.rithin the neaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision $/i1I
have a naterial financial effect, distinguishable
fron its effect on the public Aenerally, on the
official 
or a nenber of his or her inmediate fanily
or on3
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment $/orth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or Bore.
*tt
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a coumercial lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tlro hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or nore in value provided to,
received by or prornised to the public official
within 12 months Prior to the tine when the
decision is nade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official. is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
According to your facts, Councilnember Penna has a variety of
potential financial interests which nust be evaluated to deternine


Our File No. A-90-499b
Page 6
whether the councitmember has a conflict of interest. 
where any
one economic interest wiIl be foreseeably and materially affected
by a decision, the councilrnenber Dust disqualify himself.
First, any person or business that has made any palrment to
the councilneurLei in the past 12 Bonths is a source of income to
the councilnenber for the purposes of Section 87103. In addition,
Section 32030 provides that the income of an individual also
6i beneficiallv. a 1o-percent interest or qreater. 
(RugselI
eav:.ce Letter, No. A-88-484.) As sole owner of his real estate
business, all, sources of incone of S25o or nore to the business
iaa "orrr"a= of incone of S25o or :nore to the councilnember'2
Moreover, the counci lmernber' s interest in his business is a
separate and distinct economic interest that nay also.result in a
.orrfti"t of interest. 
Thus, irrespective of the receipt of anv
income, where a decision will foreseeably affect the
co.rnc i imernUer' s business, he nust disgual.ify himself' 
For
""i.pf", 
a new business which has received no incone may stiII
."rrit 
in disqualification vrhere the decision wiII foreseeablv
affect the business' access to future revenue.
2. Foreseeabi I itY
As stated above, a public officiaL is required to disqualify
hinself fron participating in a decision in which the official 
has
a financial interest. 
A financial interest exists if the
governmental decision will have a foreseeable and rnaterial
iinancial effect on an economic interest of the official. 
Whether
the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeabLe at the €ine a governEental decision is rnade depends on
the facts of each particular case. Generally, an effect. is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
Iikelihood that it will occur. Certaintv is not required'
However, if an effect is only a llere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (fn re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops' 198')
The Thorner opinion provides further guidance. In Thorner,
the uarin-Itrnic ipal water- District hras confronted lrith a decision
as to $rhether a district lrater noratorium should be lifted. 
The
removal of the moratoriura would have a dramati'c effect on
developnent projects in the district, 
and llr- Thorner asked
whethei therl would be a conflict of interest for Director Jack
' 
I., yo,r. request for reconsiderati.on you have clarified that the
deve).oper has not been a source of income to the councilnember'
Howevei, we do not have information regarding other clients of the
councilmember. Thus, this letter does not further address the
issue of potential conflicts of interest based on clients who have
been sources of income.


Our Fi■e No. A-90-499b
Page 7
MacPhail if he had bid to supply building materials for one of the
development Proj ects.
The Conmission determined that lifting 
the moratorium would
foreseeablv affect Director uacPhail's business if he had bid or
was preparing to bid on a contract with the developers to supply
mateiials, or was Iikelv to supolv naterials to the developers.
Thus, the rule set forth in Thorner is that foreseeable. effects on
a business entity in nhich an official 
has an economi'c interest is
sufficient to result in disqua I i fication, even where the effects
were not certain.
Your request for reconsideration appears to focus on this
concept of foreseeability. 
Hourever, it appears that your own
analviis of the situation blurs the Iine between foreseeability at
tne Line of a decision and the actual existence of an econonic
interest. 
So long as the councilmenber owns his business, he has
an economic interest uhj.ch nay be affected by a governnental
decision. The real question is ghether the subseouent effect on
his business, in the forn of potential increased income, is
foreseeable.
In the Armento Advice Letter we advised:
The purpose of any redevelopment plan is to
promote sound develoPment and redeveloPment of
ttigtrtea areas. The anticipated result of
redeveloprnent is an increase in property values and
an irnproved business ctimate within the proiect
area, $rhich benefits the coumunity as a whole. The
very nature of redevelopnent projects has led the
Commission to find that it is reasonably
foreseeable that there r,ill be a financial effect
on real property values and business interests
located within or near project areas. ICitations
onitted l
we also cited to the oqlesbv opinion (suDra) j.n which the
Cornrnission stated:
The eventual sale or lease transactions
involving any proPerties within or outside the area
will result in increased cornmissions, proportional
to the increased values, to Mr. whatley. There is
also a likelihood that the increased attractions
added to the downtown area and the upgrading of
other neighborhoods by new access and street
irnprovenents will result in greater numbers of
property transactions. Both these possibilities
constitute a reasonably foreseeable naterial
financial effect on Mr. Whatley's reaL estate
business, since both the size and the nunber of


Our File No. A-90-499b
Page 8
commissions generated by real estate sales in the
area are like1y to increase.
Oolesbv Opinion p. 10.
The court in Downev Cares v. Downev Commun■
tv Development
Com・ (■987) ■96 CaloApp.3d 983′
 9onsidered a similar question and
reached a consiStent conclusion。
The trial court also found the ordinance had a
foreseeable naterial effect on Mr. Santangelo's
income as a realtor, since a realtor's income is
based on a percentage of the value of property
sold, and it was reasonably foreseeable the
redeveloprnent plan r.rould increase property values
in the redeveLopnent area. Appellants point to
evidence that in his prior experience as a realtor,
Mr. santangelo dealt prirnarily with residential
property, had only one or two listings in the
redevelopnent area and no sales in the
redeveloprnent area. The trial court could discount
appellants' evidence, in light of other evidence
thit Santanqelo's prior real estate practice was at
another location outside of either redevelopment
area, he had just recentJ.y acquired the real
property in the nev redeveloPment area in April
fgea, ana the century 21 real estate franchise in
uay 1984, and his firra did not actively avoid
listings in either redevelopment area.
196 CaI.App.3d 983, at 990.
In the Artnento Advice Letter, we concluded:
councitnenber Penna's situation is somewhat
analogous to the situation discussed in oqlesbv.
councilnember Penna is the owner of a well-
established real estate coEpany which does 80t of
its business in South San Francisco. He has
indicated that he nay pursue business oPPortunities
connected with the Shearwater project. 
we have
stated in previous advice that real estate
businesses located within or near redevelopment
areas are in a unigue position to benefit from
3 whil" the Downev cares case dealt with a public official 
who
had two econoroic interests: 
(1) property interests, and,
(2) business interests, the court's reasoning did not rely on the
fact that the public official 
had economic interests in the
development area. The court analyzed the foreseeability issue
specifical.ly and soleIy with respect to effects on potential
income of the real estate business and found that those effects
lrere foreseeable.


Our Fi■e No. A-90-499b
Page 9
redevelopment decisions. These businesses, and the
professionals affiliated sith them, earn their
income from commissions based on a percentage of
the value of property soLd. It is precisely this
connectj,on between redevelopnent decisions and the
l-ikelihood of increased incone to real estate
businesses located in the area that provides the
requisite foreseeability that councj.Inember Penna
wiII benefit in a nanner distinguishable frorn the
public genera 1IY.
(Citation omitted. )
You have not provided any additional facts to indicate that
the receipt of income frorn clients in the project area and the
enhancenent of that income as a result of the redevelopment
decisions are not foreseeable. Consistent wj.th the authority
discussed above, it appears that potential financiaL effects on
the councilmenber's business are foreseeable. Thus, we confirm
this aspect of the conclusion of the Armento Advice Letter.
3. Materia I itv
In addition, the foreseeable effect on the councilnember's
financial. interests urust also be rnaterial to require disqualifica-
tion. 
The conrnission has adopted guidelines to determine whether
the effect of a governmental decision is rnaterial. The standards
vary depending on the specific circunstances of each decision'
For-exailple, where a business entity, such as the councilnenber's
real estite business, is directlv involved in a decision.before
the official's 
agency, th;-;?ficlaI 
may not participate.'
(Regulation L8702-1(a).) According to your facts, the
couicilnember's business is not directly involved in the decisions
you describe.
However, the Act also requires an official 
to disqualify
hirnself or herself from Participation in governnental decisions
which indirectlv have a uraterial financial effect on any econolnic
interest. 
whether the indirect effect of a governmental decision
on a business entitv is rnaterial, depends on the financial size of
the business entity. 
For examPle, if the councilmember's real
estate business was a relatively srnalI business entity, Regulation
4 A busin"ss entity is directly involved in a decision before the
city council when the source or an agent of the source:
(1) Initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim,
appeal, or siniLar request; or, (2) is a narned party in, or the
subject of, the proceeding. A business entity is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any J.icense, permit, or other entitlement
to, or contract rrrith, the business entity. 
(Regulation
L8702.1(b) . )

l-a702.2 (g) provides the indirect effect of a decision is material
$rhere:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $1O,000 or nore; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or elininating existing exPenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of 92,500 or nore; or
(3) The deCiSion will
or decrease in the value of
of S■0,000 。r more.
result in the increase
assets or I iabi l ities
As stated in the oqelsbv opinion, the irnprovement of the
redevelopnent area wiII result in an increase in property
iri.,sa"tions, 
and the transactions involving property within or
near the redevelopment area wiII result in increased commissions,
proportional to the increased values. Thus, absent facts to
i"aicate that the effect on the councilmenber's business will not
be material pursuant to the appropriate subdivision in Regulation
La702.2, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision to
establish the redevelopnent project area and any major decision
concerni.ng the redevelopment area or any decision that $'ould
affect th6 ultirnate =uctes= of the project will have a material
financial effect on the reaL estate business of the councilnember.
(See e.q., Gi1lio Advice Letter, No. A-88-250, copy enclosed' )
4. Public GeneralIv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financiaL effect of a
decision is naterial, disqua l if ication is required only if the
effect is distinguishable froB the effect on the public generally.
(section 87103.) You have provided infornatj.on on the poputation
of South San Francisco. You stated that the city has a population
of 52,OOO persons. Out of a total area of 9.53 square miles,
1,500 acrei in the city liruits (one-fourth of the city) is zoned
conrnercial and industrial, the rest is zoned residential'
For the ttpublic generallyrr exceptj.on to apPly to your facts,
the redevetopn-nt decision nust affect Councilnember Penna's real
estate businLss in substantial).y the same nanner as it l,ill affect
all residents of the city, or a significant segment of the
residents of the city. 
This seeus unlikely since only a sma1l
percentage of the city's residents are in the real estate business
ind few other businesses outside the project area wi]I be affected
sirnilarly. 
consequently, we confirro the conclusion in the Armento
letter that the "public generaLly" exception does not appl'y to
these facts.


Our Fi■e No. A-90-499b
Page ■■
5. other ParticiDation
You have also asked whether participation in informal city
council activities 
such as the negotiation, sale or lease of
property with a developer was subject to the same conflict of
intlresl 
rules discussed above. This issue was not discussed in
the Armento Advice Letter. Participation in governmental
decisions has been interpreted broadly in furtherance of the goals
of the Act. Participation includes voting, making an appointment,
cornrnitting an agency to a course of action, entering into a
contractuil agrienent on behatf of the agency, detennining not to
act, negotiating, advising or naking reconmendations to the
decision-maker. In addition, where a public official 
contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise atteDpts to influence, any menber,
otiicer, 
employee or consultant of the official's 
agency.
con"erni.ng i governn"ntal decision, the official 
is considered to
have used his official 
position to influence the decision.
(Regulations 18700 and 18700. 1- )
Thus, participating in the city council's negotiations and
matters con-erning sale or Iease of property with the developer
would be particip;tion 
in governmental decision. Again, if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the project would have an effect on
yorr. e"ono.ic interests. you would have a conflict of interest,
even if you do not presently have a deal pending.
Please note, that a public officiat 
is atso prohibited from
using his or her official position to. influence a decision in
wnicf the official 
has a conflict of interest. 
(Regulation
18700.1(a).) An official 
is using his or her official 
position to
influence a decision where the official 
contacts, or appears
before, or othenrise attenpts to influence, any menber, officer,
employee or consultant of the official's 
agency or any an agency
appointea by or subject to the budqetary control of his or her
agln"y. Moieover, an official 
is using his or her official
p6sition to influence a decision before any other agency if, for
th" p,r.po=" of influencing the decision, the official 
acts or
purports to act on behalf of, or as the representativg of,.his or
irer- agency. Such actions include, but are not liroited to the use
of official 
stationery. 
(Regulation 18700.1(c).)
6. Secrmentation of Decisions
As rre stated above, since the decisions concerning the
redeveloprnent project will have a foreseeable rnaterial financial
effect on the counc i l:aernber ' s real estate business, he :nay not
participate in those decisions. Holrever, j.t would aPpear fron
your facts that as the redeveloPnent project moves forward there
may le some decisions that hlill not independently affect the


Our Fi■e No. A-90-499b
page ■2
councilmenber's business and will be severable from the major
decisions for which he is disqualified.5
GeneraIIy, every governmental decision must be analyzed
independently with respect to the foreseeabj.lity and the
mateiialitv of a financial effect on an official's 
economic
interest.6- (In re Owen (].976:} 2 FPPC OPs. 77.) For example,
where the decisions are severable, such as snaller projects in a
larger project, and none of the decisions concerning the severable
parts will affect the decision on the other project for lrhich the
otticial 
is disqualified, each nro'iect nav be anaLvzed seoaratelv
to determine if the official 
has a conflict of interest. 
(Kilian
Advice Letter, No' A-89-522.)
If indeed the decisions are severable and the councilmember
deternines he has a conflict of interest rvj-th respect to some, but
not all the decisions, the following procedure should be fol'lo$/ed
to permit the councilmember to participate:
(1) The decisions for which the councilmenber has a
disquatifying financial interest should be segregated from the
other decisions.
(2) The decisions fron nhich the councilmernber is
di.squalified shouLd be considered first, 
and a final decision
reaCh"d by the city council without the councilmernber
participating in any way.
(3) once a decision has been roade on the projects for which
the councilrnenber has a dj.squalifying financial interest, he nay
participate in the deliberations regardinql the other zones, so
iong .=- those detiberations do not result in a reopening or in any
lray affect the decisions from lrhich he was disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343. )
5 Th" issue of the possible segrentation of redevelopment
decisions was not discussed in the Arnento Letter. 
This
discussion is intended to supplenent the dj.scussion in the Armento
Advice Letter.
6 Ho,rarr"a, under nany circumstances a series of decisions nay be
too interrelated to be considered separately. (Mi1l-er Advj.ce
Letter, No. A-82-119. ) For exaDple, if it is determined that an
official 
has a conflict of interest as to decisions concerning a
specific project because of the project's irnpact on an economic
interest, the official 
nay be sinilarly disqualified as to
decisions concerning the financing of that project because the
f inanci.ng decj.sion could alter the previous decision for which the
official 
was disqualified. 
(Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038.)


Our Fi■e No. A-90-499b
Page ■3
we do not have sufficient inforrnation on each decision to
corne before the city council to advise on segmentation. PLease
feel free to contact us if you believe this analysis may be
applicable to a specific decision.
7. Closed Sessions
You have asked whether councilnetnber Penna rnay be present at
either open or closed sessions of either agency when the

to preclude a disquatified councilnenber fron attending a closed
session of the city council, but did not preclude the
counc i l:nernber' s presence at an 9pg4 rneeting.
we also advised that if inforrnation in written form was
presented to the city council to enable them to discuss potential
disposition of city ProPerty and the discussion is scheduLed for a
cloied session of the city council, the Hamilton case could be
read to preclude providing the information to the disqualified
counciLmenber. However, provision of non-conf ident ia I material
scheduled for discussion at an .gpgD session of the city council to
a disqualified officiat did not appear to be prohibited by the
Hamilton case.
f trust this letter anssers your question. If you have any

sections.conclusion:
S
1. As concluded in the Armento Advice Letter, Councilmernber
Penna, as sol,e owner of his real estate business, has an econonic
interest in his business. consequently, ehere a decision vill
foreseeably affect the councilmenber's business, he must

of action, entering into a contractual agreement on behalf of the
agency, deteruining not to act, negotiating, advising or naking
reconraendations to the decision-uaker. Thus, participating- in the
city council's negotiations and Datters concerning sale or lease
of property with the developer would be participation in a
governnental decision and is prohibited if the councilrnember has a
conflict of interest.

disqualified, but is not precluded fron being present at an gpc.ll
neeting. Materials presented to the city council for a closed
session of the city council nay not be provided to a councilnember

district 
and a tandscape and Lighting maintenance district 
for

sections.analysis:
The Political Reforn Act was enacted by the people of the
State of California by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the
conf I ict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act was to ensure that
public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would perfor:n
their duties in an irnpartial nanner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons rrho have supported them. (Section 81001.(b) .) 
The Act
leeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks
to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest.
(witt v. Morrow, (L977) 7O CaI.App.3d 817, 823.)
In furtherance of this goaI, Section 871-o0 of the Act
prohibits any public official 
fron rnaking, participatj.ng in
making, or otherwise using his or her official 
position to


Our b
influence a governnental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
As discussed in our first letter to your
office, a I'public official" 
is defined in section 82048 and
Regulation lgzoo as every natural person who j.s a mernber, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.
this definition would include both redevelopment agency rnembers
and nenbers of the south San Francisco city council- (Arnento
Advice Letter, suDra. )
Please note that a conflict of interest arises r'rith respect
to a specific qovernnental decision. The Act does not restrict
councFernber ienna's private activity. 
However, as will 
be
discussed belotr, where it is foreseeable that a governmental
decision will affect an existing economic interest of the
councilnember, even in the future, a conflict of interest arises
at the time of the decision.
1. EconaEiq_l-nleEe-glg
Section 87103 Provides:
An official 
has a financial interest in a
decision r.rithin the neaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision $/i1I
have a naterial financial effect, distinguishable
fron its effect on the public Aenerally, on the
official 
or a nenber of his or her inmediate fanily
or on3
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment $/orth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or Bore.
*tt
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a coumercial lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tlro hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or nore in value provided to,
received by or prornised to the public official
within 12 months Prior to the tine when the
decision is nade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official. is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
According to your facts, Councilnember Penna has a variety of
potential financial interests which nust be evaluated to deternine


Our b
whether the councitmember has a conflict of interest. 
where any
one economic interest wiIl be foreseeably and materially affected
by a decision, the councilrnenber Dust disqualify himself.
First, any person or business that has made any palrment to
the councilneurLei in the past 12 Bonths is a source of income to
the councilnenber for the purposes of Section 87103. In addition,
Section 32030 provides that the income of an individual also
6i beneficiallv. a 1o-percent interest or qreater. 
(RugselI
eav:.ce Letter, No. A-88-484.) As sole owner of his real estate
business, all, sources of incone of S25o or nore to the business
iaa "orrr"a= of incone of S25o or :nore to the councilnember'2
Moreover, the counci lmernber' s interest in his business is a
separate and distinct economic interest that nay also.result in a
.orrfti"t of interest. 
Thus, irrespective of the receipt of anv
income, where a decision will foreseeably affect the
co.rnc i imernUer' s business, he nust disgual.ify himself' 
For
""i.pf", 
a new business which has received no incone may stiII
."rrit 
in disqualification vrhere the decision wiII foreseeablv
affect the business' access to future revenue.
2. Foreseeabi I itY
As stated above, a public officiaL is required to disqualify
hinself fron participating in a decision in which the official 
has
a financial interest. 
A financial interest exists if the
governmental decision will have a foreseeable and rnaterial
iinancial effect on an economic interest of the official. 
Whether
the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeabLe at the €ine a governEental decision is rnade depends on
the facts of each particular case. Generally, an effect. is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
Iikelihood that it will occur. Certaintv is not required'
However, if an effect is only a llere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (fn re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops' 198')
The Thorner opinion provides further guidance. In Thorner,
the uarin-Itrnic ipal water- District hras confronted lrith a decision
as to $rhether a district lrater noratorium should be lifted. 
The
removal of the moratoriura would have a dramati'c effect on
developnent projects in the district, 
and llr- Thorner asked
whethei therl would be a conflict of interest for Director Jack
' 
I., yo,r. request for reconsiderati.on you have clarified that the
deve).oper has not been a source of income to the councilnember'
Howevei, we do not have information regarding other clients of the
councilmember. Thus, this letter does not further address the
issue of potential conflicts of interest based on clients who have
been sources of income.


Our Fi■e No. A-90-499b
MacPhail if he had bid to supply building materials for one of the
development Proj ects.
The Conmission determined that lifting 
the moratorium would
foreseeablv affect Director uacPhail's business if he had bid or
was preparing to bid on a contract with the developers to supply
mateiials, or was Iikelv to supolv naterials to the developers.
Thus, the rule set forth in Thorner is that foreseeable. effects on
a business entity in nhich an official 
has an economi'c interest is
sufficient to result in disqua I i fication, even where the effects
were not certain.
Your request for reconsideration appears to focus on this
concept of foreseeability. 
Hourever, it appears that your own
analviis of the situation blurs the Iine between foreseeability at
tne Line of a decision and the actual existence of an econonic
interest. 
So long as the councilmenber owns his business, he has
an economic interest uhj.ch nay be affected by a governnental
decision. The real question is ghether the subseouent effect on
his business, in the forn of potential increased income, is
foreseeable.
In the Armento Advice Letter we advised:
The purpose of any redevelopment plan is to

on real property values and business interests
located within or near project areas. ICitations

196 CaI.App.3d 983, at 990.
In the Artnento Advice Letter, we concluded:
councitnenber Penna's situation is somewhat

councilnember Penna is the owner of a well-
established real estate coEpany which does 80t of
its business in South San Francisco. He has
indicated that he nay pursue business oPPortunities
connected with the Shearwater project. 
we have
stated in previous advice that real estate
businesses located within or near redevelopment
areas are in a unigue position to benefit from
3 whil" the Downev cares case dealt with a public official 
who
had two econoroic interests: 
(1) property interests, and,
(2) business interests, the court's reasoning did not rely on the

had economic interests in the
development area. The court analyzed the foreseeability issue

Our Fi■e No. A-90-499b
redevelopment decisions. These businesses, and the
professionals affiliated sith them, earn their
income from commissions based on a percentage of
the value of property soLd. It is precisely this

(Citation omitted. )
You have not provided any additional facts to indicate that

3. Materia I itv
In addition, the foreseeable effect on the councilnember's
financial. interests urust also be rnaterial to require disqualifica-
tion. 
The conrnission has adopted guidelines to determine whether
the effect of a governmental decision is rnaterial. The standards
vary depending on the specific circunstances of each decision'
For-exailple, where a business entity, such as the councilnenber's
real estite business, is directlv involved in a decision.before
the official's 
agency, th;-;?ficlaI 
may not participate.'
(Regulation L8702-1(a).) According to your facts, the
couicilnember's business is not directly involved in the decisions
you describe.
However, the Act also requires an official 
to disqualify
hirnself or herself from Participation in governnental decisions
which indirectlv have a uraterial financial effect on any econolnic
interest. 
whether the indirect effect of a governmental decision
on a business entitv is rnaterial, depends on the financial size of
the business entity. 
For examPle, if the councilmember's real
estate business was a relatively srnalI business entity, Regulation

Even if the reasonably foreseeable financiaL effect of a

For the ttpublic generallyrr exceptj.on to apPly to your facts,

5. other ParticiDation
You have also asked whether participation in informal city
council activities 
such as the negotiation, sale or lease of
property with a developer was subject to the same conflict of
intlresl 
rules discussed above. This issue was not discussed in
the Armento Advice Letter. Participation in governmental
decisions has been interpreted broadly in furtherance of the goals
of the Act. Participation includes voting, making an appointment,
cornrnitting an agency to a course of action, entering into a
contractuil agrienent on behatf of the agency, detennining not to
act, negotiating, advising or naking reconmendations to the
decision-maker. In addition, where a public official 
contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise atteDpts to influence, any menber,
otiicer, 
employee or consultant of the official's 
agency.
con"erni.ng i governn"ntal decision, the official 
is considered to
have used his official 
position to influence the decision.
(Regulations 18700 and 18700. 1- )
Thus, participating in the city council's negotiations and
matters con-erning sale or Iease of property with the developer
would be particip;tion 
in governmental decision. Again, if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the project would have an effect on
yorr. e"ono.ic interests. you would have a conflict of interest,
even if you do not presently have a deal pending.
Please note, that a public officiat 
is atso prohibited from
using his or her official position to. influence a decision in
wnicf the official 
has a conflict of interest. 
(Regulation
18700.1(a).) An official 
is using his or her official 
position to
influence a decision where the official 
contacts, or appears
before, or othenrise attenpts to influence, any menber, officer,
employee or consultant of the official's 
agency or any an agency
appointea by or subject to the budqetary control of his or her
agln"y. Moieover, an official 
is using his or her official
p6sition to influence a decision before any other agency if, for
th" p,r.po=" of influencing the decision, the official 
acts or
purports to act on behalf of, or as the representativg of,.his or
irer- agency. Such actions include, but are not liroited to the use
of official 
stationery. 
(Regulation 18700.1(c).)
6. Secrmentation of Decisions
As rre stated above, since the decisions concerning the

and a final decision
reaCh"d by the city council without the councilmernber

we do not have sufficient inforrnation on each decision to

7. Closed Sessions
You have asked whether councilnetnber Penna rnay be present at
either open or closed sessions of either agency when the
